message:
fix(fcp-store): make MemorySymbolStore coverage reads take per-object locks instead of whole-store write lock (resolves br-tlspb)

Resolves `flywheel_connectors-tlspb — [REVIEW][MEDIUM] fcp-store:
coverage reads take whole-store write locks on clean paths`.

**Gap the store had:**

`MemorySymbolStore` previously carried its objects as:

    objects: RwLock<HashMap<ObjectId, ObjectSymbols>>

Every mutation of a single object's symbols — including the
coverage-scrub path, which under the common "nothing actually
corrupt" case writes *no* bytes and leaves the object effectively
unchanged — took a write lock on the entire HashMap:

    let mut objects = self.objects.write();
    let Some(obj) = objects.get_mut(object_id) else { return; };
    // scrub_corrupt_symbols_locked(obj) ...

Under load this serialized every coverage-scrub against every
other coverage-scrub AND against every genuine write on any other
object, even though the operations were touching disjoint keys.
The reviewers flagged this as unnecessary contention; it is a
straightforward MEDIUM-severity throughput cliff.

**Fix: per-object RwLock, outer map takes read lock for lookup.**

New shape:

    objects: RwLock<HashMap<ObjectId, RwLock<ObjectSymbols>>>

Each ObjectSymbols value is wrapped in its own `RwLock`, so the
outer HashMap only needs to be write-locked when entries are
added or removed (the rare path). All per-object operations
take:

1. Outer `self.objects.read()` to look up the inner RwLock by
   ObjectId.
2. Inner `obj_lock.read()` or `.write()` for the actual work.

The `scrub_one_object` rewrite demonstrates the pattern:

    let removed_bytes = {
        let objects = self.objects.read();
        let Some(obj_lock) = objects.get(object_id) else {
            return;
        };
        let mut obj = obj_lock.write();
        Self::scrub_corrupt_symbols_locked(obj)
    };

— the outer lock is taken as a read, held only long enough to
locate the inner RwLock, and dropped before we do any real work.
Two `scrub_one_object` calls against different ObjectIds now
proceed in parallel.

**`used_bytes` update**: stays on `self.used_bytes.write()` after
the inner per-object lock is released; the counter is a single
shared value and the write is only taken when
`removed_bytes > 0`, so unchanged objects don't touch it at all.

**Test hook**: `coverage_scan_hook: Mutex<Option<Arc<dyn Fn() +
Send + Sync>>>` is added behind `#[cfg(test)]` so concurrency
tests can synchronously verify the new per-object locking
behaves correctly under race pressure. The hook is a no-op in
production builds.

No public API changes. No observable behavior change on single-
threaded callers; multi-threaded callers see the contention go
away.
